title: Quiz: Error Handling in Hybrid Quantum-Classical Systems

questions.question: Which type of quantum error is typically considered 'transient' and most likely to succeed on retry?

options.text: Hardware calibration drift
Circuit validation failure due to unsupported gates
Network timeout or rate limiting
Qubit decoherence during execution

questions.explanation: Network timeouts and rate limiting are transient errors that typically resolve on retry. Hardware calibration drift and qubit decoherence are persistent until recalibration, while circuit validation failures require code changes.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of adding 'jitter' to exponential backoff retry strategies?

options.text: To reduce the total number of retry attempts
To prevent the 'thundering herd' problem when multiple clients retry simultaneously
To increase the delay between retries
To improve the accuracy of quantum results

questions.explanation: Jitter adds randomness to retry delays to prevent multiple clients from retrying at exactly the same time (thundering herd problem), which would cause additional load spikes on the quantum backend.

questions.question: In the graceful degradation pattern, what happens when the system transitions from 'Reduced Quantum' to 'Hybrid Fallback' mode?

options.text: All quantum operations are disabled
Quantum resources are used only for critical computational paths
The system switches entirely to classical simulation
Error mitigation is disabled to reduce overhead

questions.explanation: In Hybrid Fallback mode, the system reserves quantum resources for critical paths only, using classical methods for less critical operations. This provides a balance between quantum benefit and system stability.

questions.question: What makes the AdaptiveRetryStrategy different from a standard ExponentialBackoffStrategy?

options.text: It uses linear delays instead of exponential
It tracks error history to make smarter retry decisions
It always retries the maximum number of times
It does not support jitter

questions.explanation: AdaptiveRetryStrategy tracks error history and patterns to make intelligent retry decisions. It can increase delays when similar errors keep occurring and avoid retrying if the same error type has failed multiple times recently.

questions.question: When should a CircuitValidationError be thrown instead of attempting retries?

options.text: When the quantum backend is temporarily unavailable
When the circuit exceeds the backend's qubit or gate constraints
When the job queue is full
When calibration data is outdated

questions.explanation: CircuitValidationError should be thrown for structural issues like circuit exceeding qubit limits or using unsupported gates. These errors require code changes rather than retries, as the same circuit will always fail validation.

questions.question: In the ClassicalFallbackRegistry, what does the 'accuracy_loss' metric indicate?

options.text: The percentage of time the fallback will fail
The expected reduction in solution quality compared to quantum execution
The increase in execution time
The memory overhead of the fallback implementation

questions.explanation: The accuracy_loss metric indicates the expected reduction in solution quality when using the classical fallback compared to the quantum algorithm. For example, 0.15 accuracy_loss means the classical solution is expected to be 15% less accurate.

questions.question: In a well-designed error handling system, HardwareError exceptions should always be marked as retryable=True because the hardware might recover after a brief wait.
questions.explanation: False. HardwareError exceptions are typically marked as retryable=False because hardware failures (calibration drift, qubit failures, system issues) usually require intervention or recalibration that takes significant time. Retrying immediately would waste resources.

questions.question: Which of the following are valid recovery actions for handling a quantum job timeout error? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Reduce the number of shots and retry
Split the execution into smaller batches
Modify the circuit's gate definitions
Switch to an alternative quantum backend
Use a classical fallback implementation
Increase the qubit count for faster execution

questions.explanation: Valid recovery actions for timeout errors include reducing shots (less work), splitting into batches (smaller jobs), switching backends (different queue), and using classical fallback. Modifying gate definitions doesn't address timeout, and increasing qubits would make execution slower, not faster.
